data.title: Boston Scientific forecasts 2026 organic growth of 6.5% to 8% amid revised outlook for EP and WATCHMAN
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Boston Scientific (BSX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "First quarter represented a solid quarter for Boston Scientific with total company organic sales growth of 9.4% versus our guidance range of 8.5% to 10%." (Chairman, President & CEO Michael Mahoney)
  • "2026 has proven to be a more challenging year than we initially expected." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "We are guiding to organic growth of 5% to 7% for the second quarter and reducing our full year guidance to 6.5% to 8%, reflecting unanticipated headwinds and changing business patterns." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "Last year, we did announce our intent to discontinue the POLARx cryo catheter, but have accelerated that timing given some recent safety events and the availability of nonthermal ablation technologies." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "Urology did have a difficult quarter in Q1 as sales grew 1% organically, falling short of our expectations, driven primarily by the stone management and sacral neuromodulation businesses." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "WATCHMAN grew 19% organically in the first quarter, which was below our expectations with pressure on volumes in the U.S. as the quarter progressed." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "Organic sales grew 22%, 18% in the U.S. and 30% internationally." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "First quarter consolidated revenue of $5.203 billion represents 11.6% reported growth versus first quarter 2025 and includes a 220 basis point tailwind from foreign exchange." (Executive VP & CFO Jonathan Monson)

Outlook

  • "We are guiding to organic growth of 5% to 7% for the second quarter" and "reducing our full year guidance to 6.5% to 8%." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "Our second quarter '26 adjusted EPS guide is $0.82 to $0.84, and we now expect our full year adjusted EPS to be $3.34 to $3.41." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "We now expect full year 2026 reported revenue growth to be in the range of 7.0% to 8.5% versus 2025" and "full year 2026 operational and organic growth to be in the range of 6.5% to 8.0%." (Executive VP & CFO Monson)
  • "We expect second quarter 2026 operational and organic growth to be in the range of 5.0% to 7.0%." (Executive VP & CFO Monson)
  • "We now expect full year 2026 adjusted earnings per share to be in the range of $3.34 to $3.41" and "we expect second quarter adjusted earnings per share to be in the range of $0.82 to $0.84." (Executive VP & CFO Monson)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management moved from "10% to 11%" full-year organic growth guidance and "$3.43 to $3.49" full-year adjusted EPS guidance to "6.5% to 8.0%" organic growth and "$3.34 to $3.41" adjusted EPS. (Executive VP & CFO Monson)

Financial Results

  • $0.80 adjusted EPS vs. the company’s guidance range of $0.78 to $0.80. (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "Adjusted gross margin for the first quarter was 70.5%, which represents a 100 basis point decline versus the first quarter of 2025, primarily driven by tariffs as well as inventory charges related to the discontinuation of our POLARx cryoablation system." (Executive VP & CFO Monson)
  • "First quarter adjusted operating margin was 28.0%." (Executive VP & CFO Monson)
  • "Free cash flow for the first quarter was $170 million" and "we now expect full year 2026 free cash flow to be approximately $4 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Monson)
  • "Our gross debt leverage ratio was 1.8x" as of March 31, 2026. (Executive VP & CFO Monson)

Q&A

  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan Chase & Co.: what changed since the Q4 guide and why confidence in the long-range plan; Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney: "there's really 3 main contributors to the takedown of the guide... primarily EP, WATCHMAN and Urology," adding WATCHMAN softness first appeared "early to mid-February" and EP reflected "greater share erosion than we're particularly seeing."
  • Joanne Wuensch, Citigroup: quarterly phasing for EP/WATCHMAN/Urology; Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney: "We think second quarter is our toughest quarter of the year"; Executive VP & CFO Monson: "Global EP at 10% for the year implies mid- to high single-digit growth for the rest of the year" and WATCHMAN implies "low double-digit growth for the rest of the year" globally.
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: EP share pressure and whether U.S. EP stays flat; Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney: U.S. EP is guided to "mid-single-digit growth" for 2026, implying "a flat 2Q to 4Q," citing "increased competition" from "Medtronic," "J&J" and "Abbott."
  • Frederick Wise, Stifel: WATCHMAN post-CHAMPION reaction and how to address concerns; Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney: "We're seeing terrific concomitant demand" but "pressure" in stand-alone, and said actions include "commercial investments" and "marketing investments"; Senior VP & Global Chief Medical Officer Ken Stein: "trial kicked, all of its primary safety and efficacy endpoints" and "It just takes time for that to play through."
  • Joshua Jennings, TD Cowen: profit offsets given mix headwinds; Executive VP & CFO Monson: "we will see less mix benefit" and described "much more restrictive spend controls" and reviewing R&D to focus on "the most impactful programs."
  • Vijay Kumar, Evercore ISI: buyback rationale and funding while Penumbra pending; Executive VP & CFO Monson: "We intend to repurchase... $2 billion worth of shares" and "We'll fund that through cash on hand."
  • Matthew O'Brien, Piper Sandler: Penumbra transaction flexibility if cash component changes; Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney: "We're really excited about that team" and said the company is "hopeful and confident" on the shareholder vote.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to skeptical, repeatedly pressing on the "takedown of the guide" and share/volume deceleration in "EP" and "WATCHMAN" (e.g., "what happened during first quarter that really prompted it?").
  • Management: slightly negative on near-term performance but confident on longer-term positioning, with Mahoney calling the revision "not my happiest moment" and "very disappointed," while also stating, "we remain convicted in the future of Boston Scientific." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • Versus the prior quarter, tone shifted from confidence in "10% plus" LRP sales to acknowledging LRP top-line pressure: "we're not going to make a comment on the LRP top line growth at this point." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance changed from Q4’s "10% to 11%" full-year 2026 organic growth to Q1’s "6.5% to 8.0%," with management explicitly attributing the revision to "EP, WATCHMAN and Urology" and citing "unanticipated headwinds." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • EP messaging shifted from Q4’s expectation to "outpace" a ~15% EP market to Q1’s expectation that "we did lose a bit more share than we anticipated" and a revised view that "we now expect our global EP business to grow approximately 10% in 2026" with U.S. "mid-single-digit" growth. (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • WATCHMAN commentary moved from Q4’s "outstanding 29% growth" to Q1’s "below our expectations" and a new full-year expectation: "we now expect global WATCHMAN growth to be mid-teens with low to mid-teens in the U.S." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Unanticipated headwinds and changing business patterns" drove the guidance reset, centered on "EP, WATCHMAN and Urology." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • WATCHMAN risks cited included "hospital capacity, related procedure prioritization and evolving reimbursement dynamics." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • EP risks included "ongoing competitive dynamics" and expected "share erosion." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • Margin risks included "tariffs" and "inventory charges related to the discontinuation of our POLARx cryoablation system," with full-year gross margin expected "slightly below" 2025. (Executive VP & CFO Monson)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as on-plan operationally (9.4% organic growth and $0.80 adjusted EPS) but reset 2026 expectations, guiding Q2 organic growth of 5% to 7% and full-year organic growth of 6.5% to 8% alongside $3.34 to $3.41 in adjusted EPS. Executives tied the revision primarily to WATCHMAN volume softness that emerged in mid-February, greater-than-expected EP share pressure amid intensifying competition, and slower Urology performance linked to stone management gaps and lingering sacral neuromodulation commercial disruption, while emphasizing actions spanning spend controls, targeted commercial and marketing investment, and continued commitment to the pipeline and planned Penumbra close in the second half of 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Fundraising by direct lenders hits a three-year low in Q1
data.description:
Direct lending strategies saw their slowest fundraising activity in at least three years during the first quarter amid chaos in the private credit sector and Middle East war concerns, according to data from With Intelligence by S&P Global.

The lenders recorded $10.7B of final closes during the three months ended March 31, ~63% below the quarterly average of $28.7B seen between 2023 and 2025.

Comparatively, a total of $49.9B of private debt fundraising was tracked by With Intelligence in Q1.

Several funds delayed their first or final closes in recent weeks over market volatility resulting from the Iran conflict, the April 2 report noted, citing market participants.

Notable direct lending funds: (MSDL), (NCDL), (OBDC), (SAR)

data.title: Sportradar falls after Muddy Waters short call
data.description:
Sportadar (SRAD) plunged 24% after a short report from Muddy Waters Research. 

Muddy Waters disclosed that it's short shares of Sportradar.

Sportradar didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's email request for comment.

Sportradar (SRAD) has a short interest of 4.9%.

data.description: • Metso Oyj press release (OUKPF): Q1 Sales increased by 3% Y/Y to EUR 1.25B (EUR 1.21M).

data.title: Blackstone Q1 earnings: Real estate strength, private credit in spotlight
data.description:
When Blackstone (BX) announces first-quarter earnings on Thursday, Wall Street expects the private equity giant to post an EPS of $1.34, implying a 22.9% increase, while revenue is expected to rise 4.3% to $3.40B for the quarter.

Last quarter, the alternative asset manager turned in better-than-expected Q4 earnings on the back of increased inflows, better-than-expected fee-related earnings, and continued growth in management and advisory fees.

During its Q4 earnings call, Blackstone CFO Michael Chae said that the firm expects management fees to continue on a strong positive trajectory in 2026, underpinned by robust growth in private equity credit, insurance, and multi-asset investing segments, with real estate management fees remaining consistent with Q4 levels in the near term.

However, the alternative asset manager’s shares are down nearly 15% year-to-date, underperforming the benchmark S&P 500, which has gained 3.2% in the same period.

Earlier this month, Oppenheimer upgraded private equity giant Blackstone (BX) to Outperform from Perform as the stock has recently taken a "drubbing," despite its "outstanding performance," especially in its real estate arm.

Analyst Chris Kotowski said that a distinctive feature of BX is its real estate business, which until recently was its largest generator of fees.

“This real estate business, in our view, is without peer (with Brookfield a somewhat distant #2) and represents significant upside in the coming years,” Kotowski added.

Seeking Alpha analysts and Wall Street analysts are bullish and consider it a buying opportunity as the stock is down despite record earnings last quarter and resilient fundamentals.

Seeking Alpha analyst Pacifica Yield rated the stock a Buy, saying that redemption pressures in BX's private credit funds are being driven by sentiment, not an actual spike in non-performing loans or defaults.

“Despite market fears around private credit defaults and redemptions, BX's AUM and fee-earning AUM continue to grow, with fee-earning AUM growing by 11% year-over-year to $921.7B,” the analyst highlighted.

Over the last two years, BX has beaten EPS estimates 88% of the time and revenue estimates 50% of the time.

Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen no upward revisions and 11 downward revisions, while revenue estimates have seen no upward revisions against two downward moves.

data.title: Marijuana MSOs surge on report Trump set to formally reclassify cannabis
data.description:
• President Trump will reschedule marijuana as a Schedule III drug under the Controlled Substances Act (CSA), a major move that would allow for greater research of the plant, make it easier for pharma companies to develop cannabis-based medicines, and reduce some legal penalties associated with marijuana possession. 
  • Axios, citing an administration official, said the reclassification could come as soon as Wednesday.
  • Multi-state operators are surging on the report. 
  • Trump's decision, however, would fall short of legalizing marijuana on the federal level. Its manufacture, distribution, and possession would still be considered illegal under the CSA.
  • In December, Trump said he was "very strongly" looking at an Executive Order to reclassify marijuana. 
  • Multi-state operators: Ascend Wellness (OTCQX:AAWH), Curaleaf Holdings (OTCPK:CURLF), Cresco Labs (OTCQX:CRLBF), Green Thumb Industries (OTCQX:GTBIF), Trulieve Cannabis (OTCQX:TCNNF), Ayr Wellness (OTCQX:AYRWF), Verano Holdings (VRNO), and Jushi Holdings (OTCQX:JUSHF).
  • Cannabis ETFs: AdvisorShares Pure Cannabis ETF (NYSEARCA:YOLO), Amplify Alternative Harvest ETF (MJUS), and Amplify Seymour Cannabis ETF (NYSEARCA:CNBS).

data.title: Convenience store chain Yesway rallies in opening IPO trading
data.description:
Yesway (YSWY) opened for trading at $22 per share after pricing its IPO at $20 per share, which was the low end of the original range. The stock was swapping hands as high as $22.75 before settling back a bit. The market cap on Yesway (YSWAY) is close to $1.4B after the company raised $280M with the offering.

Yesway (YSWAY) calls itself one of the fastest-growing convenience store operators in the US, with 419 company-operated stores as of December 31, with 90 successful openings of new stores from 2020 to 2025. The geographic footprint consists of stores located in attractive rural and suburban markets across the Southwest and Midwest under the Yesway and Allsup's brand names. The greatest concentration of stores is in Texas, which has recorded the largest absolute population increase in the U.S. since 2020.

The company generated revenue of $2.67B in calendar year 2025 (+5% Y/Y), adjusted EBITDA of $187M, and net income of $54M.

Yesway (YSWY) was founded in 2015 and backed by Brookwood Financial Partners. It grew rapidly through both new-store development and acquisitions, most notably the 2019 purchase of Allsup’s, which strengthened its presence in the Southwest and expanded its foodservice business. Yesway (YSWY) operates more than 444 locations across nine states and continues to focus on store expansion, remodels, fuel upgrades, and operational improvements.

Major competitors include 7-Eleven, Casey’s General Stores (CASY), Murphy USA (MUSA), QuikTrip, and Alimentation Couche-Tard's (ATD:CA) (ANCTF) (ALCTY) Circle K.

data.title: Intel Q1 Earnings Preview: Server CPUs seen as key driver, but foundry, margin pressures remain
data.description:
Intel (INTC) is scheduled to announce Q1 earnings results on Thursday, April 23rd, after market close.

Ahead of the earnings, HSBC issued an upgrade on Tuesday as the investment firm called the continued demand for server CPUs a “bigger catalyst” for the semiconductor giant than its foundry push.

Analyst Frank Lee expects upside to Intel's server CPU business to start in the second-quarter, as he expects the company's revenue to be $14.2B, 9% above the Wall Street estimate. Further boosting Intel's results could be price hikes that aid its gross margins, Lee added.

In the same week, BNP Paribas upgraded the semiconductor firm ahead of its Q1 earnings, with analyst David O'Connor saying that Intel is likely to benefit from increased server CPU demand caused by agentic artificial intelligence.

Stifel, meanwhile, said in an earnings preview that Intel (INTC) enters with an improving narrative, server CPU demand well above expectations, and the September Nvidia (NVDA) partnership validating the x86 ecosystem, but gross margins are likely to remain static in low-to-mid 30s% in the near term and foundry losses remain a headwind.

More recent SA contributor analyses have been bearish of the stock, with Doug Collins noting that recent high-profile deals with Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN) and the Terafab project (TSLA) validate Intel’s technology but lack near-term revenue impact or clear financial details, and the foundry segment remains “deeply unprofitable.”

The Value Portfolio also sees Intel facing intense competition from TSMC, Samsung, and new entrants, and despite progress with 18A/14A nodes and partnerships like Nvidia, the foundry division posts ~$10B in annualized losses, and revenue/margins continue to decline Y/Y.

The consensus EPS estimate is $0.01 (-92.3% Y/Y) and the consensus revenue estimate is $12.43B (-2.1% Y/Y).

Over the last 2 years, INTC has beaten EPS estimates 63% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 75% of the time.

Over the last 3 months, EPS estimates have seen 8 upward revisions and 22 downward. Revenue estimates have seen 11 upward revisions and 20 downward.

data.title: Exxon weighing sale of Hong Kong gas stations for ~$500M - Bloomberg
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (XOM) is exploring a sale of its gasoline station network in Hong Kong in a deal that could fetch $500M-$600M for the assets, Bloomberg reported Wednesday, as the company continues to trim its retail footprint globally.

The potential sale of the 39 Esso-branded service stations comes at a time of uncertainty for the retail fuel industry, as the business faces renewed pressure from extreme volatility in crude markets following the outbreak of the Middle East war.

Hong Kong gasoline prices are among the highest in the world, averaging more than $15/gal, and the local government has been encouraging a switch to electric vehicles.

Exxon (XOM) has a global strategy to streamline assets and has appointed advisors to handle sales of its downstream retail sites under the Esso brand in markets including France, New Zealand, and Hong Kong, according to the report; in October, the company agreed to sell its Esso-branded service stations in Singapore to PT Chandra Asri Pacific.
